authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — OET Speaking Practice Rulebook for Healthcare Professionals (Dentistry) v1

rules.title: Open with greeting + identity + role
rules.body: Every OET role-play opens with a greeting, your name, and your role. Example: 'Good morning. My name is Dr. Ahmed, one of the doctors here today. I understand you're Mrs Patel — is that right?'
exemplarPhrases: Good morning, I'm Dr. Ahmed, one of the doctors on the ward today.
Hello, I'm one of the GPs here. Are you Mrs Jones?

rules.title: Elicit before explaining
rules.body: Use ICE (Ideas, Concerns, Expectations) before you give any explanation. Ask at least one open question about what the patient already knows or worries about.
exemplarPhrases: What do you understand about the results so far?
What's worrying you most at the moment?

rules.title: Explain in plain English
rules.body: Never use unexplained medical jargon with patients. When you must use a term, define it immediately. 'Your thyroid — that's the gland at the front of your neck — is underactive.'
exemplarPhrases: Hypertension means your blood pressure is higher than we'd like.
An ECG is a quick heart tracing — painless and takes about five minutes.

rules.title: Check understanding (teach-back)
rules.body: After explaining, invite the patient to repeat in their own words. 'Just so I know I've explained clearly, could you tell me in your own words what this means for you?'
exemplarPhrases: Just to check I've explained this well — what will you tell your partner when you get home?

rules.title: Close with plan + safety-net
rules.body: Never close without: (a) a clear next step, (b) when to return / what to watch for, (c) confirming any questions.
exemplarPhrases: Is there anything else you'd like to ask before you go?
If the pain comes back or you develop a fever, I want you back in the same day.

rules.title: Acknowledge emotion before information
rules.body: When a patient expresses worry, fear, frustration, or sadness, acknowledge it before moving to information. Never bulldoze emotion with data.
exemplarPhrases: That sounds really worrying — I can hear how anxious this has made you.
I'm sorry you're going through this.

rules.title: Use the patient's name
rules.body: Use the patient's name at least twice in a 5-minute encounter. Personalises the conversation; avoids 'the patient' as running label.

rules.title: Formal spoken register
rules.body: Professional but warm. No slang, no casual fillers beyond natural hesitation. 'Yeah' is acceptable once; 'gonna' / 'wanna' are not.

rules.title: Mirror the patient's language
rules.body: If the patient calls it 'the jab', you may say 'vaccination (or jab, as you called it)' — this shows listening without adopting slang uncritically.

rules.title: Ping-pong turn structure
rules.body: OET role-plays are conversations, not monologues. No learner turn should exceed ~120 consecutive words before inviting a response.

rules.title: Invite the patient in
rules.body: Use phrases that hand the turn back to the patient: 'Does that make sense so far?', 'What are your thoughts on that?', 'Is there anything I haven't covered?'
exemplarPhrases: Does that make sense so far?
What are your thoughts on that option?

rules.title: No interruption during patient emotion
rules.body: Do not interrupt the patient when they are expressing distress. Wait for a pause, then acknowledge.

rules.title: Start with open questions
rules.body: Begin history-taking with open questions. 'Tell me more about the pain', not 'Is it sharp?'
exemplarPhrases: Tell me a bit more about how this started.
Can you describe the pain in your own words?

rules.title: Narrow to closed questions
rules.body: After open questions, narrow with closed questions to confirm specifics (onset, duration, severity, triggers, relievers).

rules.title: Avoid leading questions
rules.body: Do not lead the patient: 'You don't drink, do you?' Instead: 'How much alcohol, if any, do you drink in a typical week?'

rules.title: One question at a time
rules.body: Avoid stacking multiple questions in one turn — patients lose track and give incomplete answers.

rules.title: Signpost transitions
rules.body: Signpost when you change topic: 'Thank you. Now I'd like to ask about your medical history.' Signposting is rewarded heavily in OET Appropriateness.
exemplarPhrases: Thank you for that. I'd like to move on to…
Before I examine you, let me just ask a few quick questions about…

rules.title: Chunk information
rules.body: Deliver explanations in chunks of 2–3 sentences, then pause for the patient to absorb or ask.

rules.title: Use analogies
rules.body: Analogies help patients grasp clinical concepts. 'Think of the arteries like pipes — when the pressure is too high for too long, the pipes get damaged.'

rules.title: Natural speech rate 130–160 wpm
rules.body: Target 130–160 words per minute. Too fast = unintelligible; too slow = patronising.

rules.title: Link function words
rules.body: Use natural connected speech: 'gotta-go' (going to go), 'wanna' (want to) are too informal; but 'I'll', 'you're', 'we've' are appropriate in speech (different from written OET).

rules.title: Use sentence stress on key words
rules.body: Stress the key clinical word in each sentence so the patient cannot miss it. 'The main concern is your BLOOD PRESSURE, not your cholesterol.'

rules.title: Contractions OK in speech
rules.body: Contractions (I'll, you're, we've) are NATURAL in spoken English and EXPECTED in OET speaking. Do NOT carry the written-letter no-contractions rule into speech.

rules.title: Conditionals for uncertainty
rules.body: Use first and second conditionals to express uncertainty: 'If the pain returns, we would need to investigate further.'
exemplarPhrases: If your symptoms worsen, please come back.
I'd suggest we start with a blood test.

rules.title: Modal verbs for softening
rules.body: Use modals (could, might, would, may) to soften instructions and options. 'You might want to try…' not 'You must try…'
exemplarPhrases: You could consider reducing your salt intake.
We might need a scan to rule this out.

rules.title: Avoid the passive for instructions
rules.body: Instructions to the patient should be in the active voice and second person: 'Take this tablet twice a day', not 'The tablet is to be taken twice a day'.

rules.title: Stay in role
rules.body: The candidate is the doctor; the interlocutor is the patient (or colleague for handover). Never break character to discuss the exam, ask about scoring, or comment meta-linguistically.

rules.title: Respect scenario objectives
rules.body: Every scenario lists objectives the candidate must cover. All must be addressed before closing.

rules.title: No personal medical opinions to the examiner
rules.body: Do not turn to the examiner/interlocutor to ask 'is that right?' — stay in the consultation.

rules.title: Adapt to patient's emotional state
rules.body: If the patient is anxious, slow down, use more empathy. If calm, can move faster through facts.

rules.title: Red-flag escalation
rules.body: If the patient describes red-flag symptoms (e.g. chest pain radiating to arm, worst headache ever, sudden weakness), you MUST acknowledge and escalate in-scenario: 'That sounds serious, I want to assess you straight away.'

rules.title: Safety-net before closing
rules.body: Always state explicit safety-netting before closing: 'If X happens, come back immediately' / 'If Y, call 999.'
exemplarPhrases: If the pain returns or you become breathless, please dial 999.
If you notice any blood in the stool, come back the same day.

rules.title: ISBAR — Identify
rules.body: Begin handover with Identify: your name, role, the patient's name, and the ward/location. 'Hi, I'm Dr. Ahmed, the doctor on call. I'm handing over Mrs Miller in Bed 4, Ward 12.'
exemplarPhrases: I'm Dr. Ahmed, the night doctor. I'd like to hand over Mr Patel in Bay 3.

rules.title: ISBAR — Situation
rules.body: State the current situation in one sentence. 'Mrs Miller is a 72-year-old lady admitted last night with shortness of breath, currently stable on 4L oxygen.'

rules.title: ISBAR — Background
rules.body: Relevant history: admission reason, key co-morbidities, key medications, relevant investigations already done.

rules.title: ISBAR — Assessment
rules.body: Your clinical assessment: vital signs, current problem list, and what you think is going on. 'My assessment is that this is most likely a congestive heart failure exacerbation.'

rules.title: ISBAR — Recommendation
rules.body: Clear recommendation / ask of the receiving clinician. 'I'd like you to review her fluid balance overnight and repeat the chest X-ray in the morning.'
exemplarPhrases: Could you please review the repeat troponin at 0600?
I'd recommend continuing hourly obs and escalating if the respiratory rate exceeds 24.

rules.title: Invite questions at the end
rules.body: Handover closes with: 'Do you have any questions?' Then document handover.
exemplarPhrases: Any questions before I go?

rules.title: Limit filler words
rules.body: Minimise 'um', 'uh', 'like', 'you know'. Natural speech has some; excessive fillers harm Fluency scoring.

rules.title: Prefer strategic pauses over fillers
rules.body: A brief pause is better than 'um'. Use pauses to gather thoughts; patients experience pauses as professional confidence.

rules.title: Summarise the plan before closing
rules.body: Summarise the plan in 2–3 sentences: what was discussed, what will happen next, what the patient should do.
exemplarPhrases: So to recap: I'll refer you for an ultrasound, you'll start the iron tablets today, and we'll review in two weeks.

rules.title: Offer written information
rules.body: Offer written information where appropriate: 'I'll give you a leaflet on this to take home.'
